autom8y::autom8y-sms::design-constraints.domain_summary: The autom8y-sms package is a Twilio-based SMS conversation orchestrator with scheduling capabilities, featuring Claude-powered tool dispatch and reminder workflows. This design constraints document catalogs structural tensions arising from dual orchestrators, optional scheduling injection, protocol bypasses, and config fragmentation across three incompatible classes. LOAD-BEARING-CODE-IDENTIFICATION: _dispatch_tool_call is the central tool routing method (5 tools) whose signature changes require coordinated updates across handlers, tests, models, and instrumentation code. EVOLUTION-CONSTRAINT-DOCUMENTATION: Adding new scheduling tools requires synchronized edits to three locations (definitions, validation allowlist, dispatch chain) with no registry automation; timezone handling raises ValueError on absence rather than defaulting, blocking future UTC fallback; prompt versions must be manually bumped without automated enforcement. RISK-ZONE-MAPPING: SmsDataClient.get_conversation_context double-parses interop responses into SMS-specific models on every call; _dispatch_tool_call lacks a test that asserts ALLOWED_TOOL_NAMES equals defined tools; get_business() bypasses the interop protocol via duck-typed _get_http_client() inspection; ConversationOrchestrator carries 7 type:ignore suppressions due to optional scheduling_client field. KNOWLEDGE-GAPS: No documented rationale for why ConversationOrchestrator and ReminderOrchestrator share TwilioClient but no base class; system_prompt field in SmsServiceConfig is defined but never read by orchestrator; circuit breaker thresholds hardcoded in TwilioClient._build_http_client with no override mechanism. TENSION-CATALOG-COMPLETENESS: Dual orchestrators (ConversationOrchestrator vs ReminderOrchestrator) lack shared interface despite shared dependencies; optional scheduling_client field creates two semantic modes in one class; PILOT_OFFICE_PHONES gate enforced separately in handler and orchestrator; SmsDataClient.get_business() bypasses interop protocol via private accessor inspection; BusinessInfo defined in conversation.py despite implying conversation-specific scope; DataServiceClient alias retained without deprecation timeline; ConversationOrchestrator imports conversation_id_var from handler layer violating service layering; console/_genai_attrs.py is a load-bearing shim awaiting upstream autom8y_telemetry.genai evolution; get_conversation_context re-parses interop response models to inject twil_phone; Config split across three classes with incompatible env-prefix conventions (TWILIO_, SMS_, REMINDER_). TRADE-OFF-DOCUMENTATION: Dual orchestrators persist because reminder and conversation workflows differ structurally (EventBridge-batch vs webhook-Claude-loop); optional scheduling client persists per ADR-007 to avoid constructor refactoring during pilot; double pilot gate persists as defense-in-depth for distinct purposes (which conversations to process vs which tools to inject); protocol bypass for get_business persists because autom8y-interop protocol lacks business record support; handler-to-service import persists because correlation ID is a ContextVar requiring per-invocation handler setup. ABSTRACTION-GAP-MAPPING: No shared scheduling client lifecycle interface despite both orchestrators calling resolve_scheduling_client() with overlapping parameters; tool dispatch lacks a registry abstraction instead using if/elif chain requiring edits to three locations for new tools; formatted result types duplicated as Pydantic models that document but never instantiate actual dict payloads; prompt version management is manual without automated enforcement; console/_genai_

autom8y::autom8y-sms::release/history.domain_summary: autom8y-sms release history documentation tracking patch releases, deployment attempts, and resolution of import and type-checking failures across a 4-stage CI/CD pipeline that includes satellite dispatch, ECR containerization, and Lambda deployment. release-log | Version 0.1.0 patch release required 3 attempts to resolve import errors (missing autom8y_telemetry.genai in v0.5.2) and mypy strict type-ignore annotations, ultimately passing all 4 CI pipeline stages (CI → Satellite Dispatch → Docker/ECR → Lambda) with environment variable standardization and SMS console module additions.

autom8y::autom8y-sms::release/platform-p.domain_summary: autom8y-sms is a Python 3.12+ SMS service package (v0.1.0) distributed as a container using hatchling and UV package management with no downstream dependencies. The project uses a 4-stage cross-repository dispatch chain that triggers from main branch pushes/PRs, orchestrating CI testing, satellite dispatch, and Lambda deployment via Terraform with build attestation (SBOM + Trivy scanning). repository-ecosystem-map | Python UV project at v0.1.0 distributed as a container with pyproject.toml manifest and no dependent packages in the organization. build-tooling | justfile provides test, lint, format, build, and deployment targets with Dockerfile for containerized distribution. pipeline-chain-discovery | Four-stage dispatch chain spanning autom8y-sms and autom8y repositories, triggered by main branch pushes/PRs, executing test → satellite dispatch → repository dispatch → Lambda deployment workflow via Terraform (~5 minute duration). configuration-artifacts | CI delegates to autom8y reusable workflows via workflow_call with satellite-deploy repository_dispatch event, Lambda-only deployment (ECS skipped), and build attestation running SBOM + Trivy scanning before Terraform apply.

autom8y::autom8y-sms::scar-tissue.domain_summary: autom8y-sms uses a comprehensive scar-tissue documentation system to track and remediate failure modes discovered in production, testing, and CI workflows. This knowledge domain catalogs defects, scars, and workarounds across configuration management, API resilience, tool validation, model serialization, and prompt guardrails, with specific commit references and remediation markers embedded in code. category-coverage | Scars span six categories: singleton/state management (SCAR-011, SCAR-014, config env var collision), API resilience (DEF-2, DEF-3, direction type relaxation), tool validation and security (DEFECT-01, DEFECT-02, DEFECT-03, prompt guardrails), CI/build reproducibility (uv export flags, Docker builds, SHA sourcing), dependency/deprecation (datetime.utcnow, Pydantic forward references, genai shim), and infrastructure gaps (trace context propagation). fix-location-mapping | Fixes are concentrated in three locations: `tests/conftest.py` (autouse fixture resetting config and logger state), `src/autom8_sms/tools/validation.py` (business hours and temporal bounds guards), and model files (`conversation.py`, `console/_models.py`) handling schema relaxation; CI workflows and prompts contain scars marked via commit messages and inline CRITICAL comments rather than code markers. defensive-pattern-documentation | The codebase employs autouse pytest fixtures for state isolation, `try/except ValidationError` wrapping around Pydantic operations, `.get()` safe dict access instead of hard keys, raised `ValueError` for missing required config, `# noqa: TC003` suppression for unavoidable non-TYPE_CHECKING imports, and explicit bounds checks (MAX_LOOKFORWARD_DAYS) on user-supplied values. agent-relevance-tagging | Scars are tagged with marker conventions `SCAR-N`, `DEF-N`, `DEFECT-N`, `WP-*` (workaround/known limitation), `SD-*` (security design), `SEC-*` (security), and `REQ-*` (requirement); markers appear in git commit messages, inline code comments, docstrings, and dedicated test modules (`test_defect_remediation.py`, `test_pilot_config.py`, `test_adversarial_tool_use.py`). knowledge-gaps | DEFECT-03 (`end_datetime` validation) remains open; WP-TEL-SMS (trace context propagation) is documented as unresolved and delegated to infrastructure; CRITICAL prompt behavior guardrails lack automated test coverage and rely on manual prompt review and production monitoring. failure-catalog-completeness | The catalog documents 20+ failure modes with evidence from git history, code markers, test assertions, and module-level docstrings; each scar includes a four-part narrative (what failed, when, how fixed, marker today) enabling future engineers to understand root causes and prevent regression without duplicating investigative work.

autom8y::autom8y-sms::test-coverage.domain_summary: autom8y::autom8y-sms test coverage characterizes the SMS service's unit and integration test structure, identifying coverage gaps in critical Lambda handlers and orchestrator error paths while documenting comprehensive negative-test coverage for clients, validation, and tool dispatch. The test suite follows strict conventions for async fixtures via pytest-asyncio, parametrized and explicit test methods, multi-level fixture hierarchies, and programmatic factory-based test data, with defensive test patterns explicitly tracking regressions from historical bugs (SCAR references).

section_summaries.coverage-gaps: `handlers/appointment_reminder.py` lambda_handler and `handlers/client_lead.py` process_pending_conversations() loop lack direct unit tests despite high production criticality, while error paths in `ConversationOrchestrator.process_conversation()` and `console/_session.py` session management are only indirectly tested via integration tests.
section_summaries.negative-test-coverage: Comprehensive error-path testing covers Twilio client 4xx/5xx/timeout failures, tool validation rejection (past-dates, malformed formats, business-hours mismatches), adversarial prompt injection attempts, data-service 404/null/malformed responses, and defect regression cases (DEF-2 model validation, DEF-3 key access guards).
section_summaries.test-structure-summary: Test data is entirely programmatic via factory hierarchy (`helpers.py` base domain factories, `factories/scheduling.py` scheduling-specific, `integration/helpers.py` integration-level) with autouse `_clean_sms_env` fixture clearing prefixed env vars and resetting config singleton before every test for universal isolation.
section_summaries.testing-conventions: Test functions use class-based grouping (`class Test{Subject}:{def test_...}`) as dominant pattern with async tests via `async def` without decorators under `asyncio_mode = "auto"`, parametrization via `@pytest.mark.parametrize` in narrative tests, and multi-level fixtures from conftest.py, SDK entry points, and test modules with monkeypatch for env-var control.

autom8y::autom8y::architecture.domain_summary: Autom8y is a Python monorepo managed with `uv` workspaces containing 20 independently-versioned SDK packages, 13 deployable service packages, and supporting infrastructure code for a multi-tenant automation platform. The architecture enforces a strict layered dependency DAG (Foundation → Transport → Cross-cutting → Domain clients → Services) with no circular imports, using Ruff TID251 to ban direct third-party imports in services and `uv` workspace constraints to enforce version floors.

section_summaries.data-flow: ECS FastAPI services receive HTTP requests routed to handler functions that invoke SDK domain clients (autom8y-meta, autom8y-stripe, autom8y-gcal, autom8y-interop) for external API calls; Lambda services consume EventBridge scheduled events, SNS notifications, or Step Functions state machine outputs, invoke domain logic, and
section_summaries.entry-points-and-api-surface: ECS FastAPI services (auth, ads) use lifespan factories to initialize singletons and mount routers exposing auth endpoints (/auth/register, /auth/login, /api-keys, /rbac/assign, /.well-known/jwks.json), internal service-token endpoints, and health/readiness probes; Lambda services use event-driven handlers (sns_to_slack_alert, contente_onboarding_handler, calendly_intake, validate_business, auth_mysql_sync) dispatched via EventBridge, SNS, or Step Functions state machines.
section_summaries.key-abstractions: Core abstractions include `Autom8yBaseSettings` for Pydantic configuration with SSM/SecretsManager resolution, `TokenManager` and `Client` for authenticated HTTP with Bearer injection, `Autom8yHttpClient` with rate limiting/retry/circuit breaker/concurrency control, multi-backend `CacheProvider` (in-memory, Redis, S3, tiered), `DomainEvent` for EventBridge publishing, typed inter-service clients via `autom8y-interop` (data, ads, asana protocols), and `ReconciliationReportBuilder`/`UnifiedVerdict` for three-way reconciliation patterns.
section_summaries.layer-boundaries: The dependency DAG enforces four SDK layers with unidirectional imports: Foundation (autom8y-config, autom8y-devx-types, autom8y-saga with zero or external-only dependencies), Transport (autom8y-http, autom8y-core, autom8y-log), Cross-cutting (autom8y-telemetry, autom8y-reconciliation, autom8y-cache, autom8y-events, autom8y-auth), and Domain clients (autom8y-meta, autom8y-stripe, autom8y-gcal, autom8y-interop, etc.), with services consuming from all SDK tiers via explicit `pyproject.toml` declarations and no wildcard version ranges.
section_summaries.package-structure: The monorepo organizes into Tier 1 (20 independently-versioned SDK packages in `sdks/python/` published to AWS CodeArtifact), Tier 2 (13 deployable services in `services/` classified by archetype: ecs-fargate-rds, ecs-fargate-stateless, lambda-scheduled, lambda-event-driven), and Tier 3 (templates, tooling, infrastructure, and JavaScript/TypeScript web properties).

autom8y::autom8y::conventions.domain_summary: This knowledge domain captures autom8y's standardized conventions for Python 3.11+ async-first SDKs and services, covering error handling hierarchies, file organization patterns, and naming rules that enforce consistency across 21 SDKs and multiple service repositories. The codebase uses `src/` layouts, dedicated `errors.py` files with `ClassVar` metadata, mixin-based resource composition, and structured keyword logging to ensure type safety, observability, and maintainability at scale. error-handling-style | Every SDK defines a dedicated `errors.py` with a base `{Package}Error` class containing `code` and `http_status` ClassVars, uses `raise X from e` cause-chaining at boundary transitions, and logs with structured keyword arguments rather than f-strings. file-organization | All 21 SDKs follow identical `src/autom8y_{name}/` layouts with `__init__.py`, `config.py`, `errors.py`, `client.py`, `models.py`, and a mandatory `testing/` subpackage; private files use `_` prefix and multi-resource clients split capabilities into `resource_{name}.py` mixin files. domain-specific-idioms | SDKs enforce hard boundaries requiring `autom8y_log.get_logger(__name__)` and `autom8y_http.Autom8yHttpClient` over raw `loguru`/`httpx`, mandate `async with` context managers for all clients, use `StrEnum` for constants, and apply `@instrument_lambda` decorator with module-level singleton clients for warm starts. naming-patterns | SDKs use `autom8y-{name}` (kebab) / `autom8y_{name}` (snake) while services use `autom8_{name}` (intentionally missing the `y`); classes follow `{Domain}Error`, `Settings`, `{Domain}Client`, and `{Noun}` patterns; auth keys derive from `{service}-service` (kebab) with SM path `autom8y/auth/service-api-keys/{db-key-name}`. knowledge-gaps | No explicit rule document exists for async vs sync conventions, creating potential ambiguity in pattern application across the codebase.

autom8y::autom8y::design-constraints.domain_summary: # autom8y Design Constraints Summary This domain catalogues 25+ architectural tensions, load-bearing constraints, and evolution risks across a Python monorepo of 21+ SDKs and 13+ services, with particular focus on legacy naming conventions, mothballed code coupled to Alembic migrations, stub implementations blocking on future work, and frozen cryptographic constants that enable deterministic UUID generation. ---

section_summaries.abstraction-gap-mapping: Identifies missing abstractions (shared Redis pool, real DataServiceClient/MetaPlatformAdapter, formal ADR for auth rename), premature abstractions (8-line AdFactory, unreachable DataSchedulingProtocol, unused CompensationRegistry), and duplication hotspots (TokenManager in autom8y-auth and autom8y-core, _compat.py in two SDKs, client lazy-init globals across contente-onboarding handlers).
section_summaries.evolution-constraint-documentation: NHC MySQL schema constraints field names externally, Meta Ads API protocol shape is dictated, AWS CodeArtifact hardcodes workspace URL, AWS Step Functions ASL couples SagaContext schema stability, GCal DWD scope mandates two-tenant architecture (thenaturalhealthcompany.org + contenteapp.com), and scheduling service removal obsoleted 6+ tensions (P015-P020) tied to notification dispatch and APScheduler.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: No content provided in source material for this section.
section_summaries.load-bearing-code-identification: Documents 7+ critical patterns whose naive removal silently breaks systems: GUID_NAMESPACE frozen UUID v5 seeding, _guard_production_urls() prevents LOCAL/TEST hitting production, JWT_ALGORITHM ClassVar defeats algorithm confusion, DB engine module-level singleton preserves connection pooling, incremental sync deactivation exclusion prevents membership corruption, audit log immutability triggers block audit mutation, and fail-open Redis policy trades durability for availability.
section_summaries.risk-zone-mapping: No content provided in source material for this section.
section_summaries.tension-catalog: Domain contains 25 documented tensions spanning naming mismatches (bare `src` package in auth, `autom8-ads` vs `autom8y-*`, new services with plain names), under-engineered stubs (LaunchService unwired, StubDataServiceClient in production), dual-system patterns (3 independent Redis pools, sync/async DB session hybrid), mothballed code on disk (credential vault, charter, OAuth routes referenced by Alembic), and schema coupling (SagaContext extra="forbid" tightly bound to AWS Step Functions ASL definitions).
section_summaries.trade-off-documentation: Current state of 10+ key tensions documents why each persists: P001 auth imports blocked by Alembic+Docker coupling, P004/P005 stub clients await Move 4 delivery, P006 triple Redis pools stem from independent feature implementation, P007 hybrid DB sessions track ADR-ASYNC-DB-001 migration, P021 intentional SagaContext typo guard via extra="forbid", and P025 calendly-intake idempotency prioritizes availability over guarantee via fail-open Redis.

autom8y::autom8y::env-architecture.domain_summary: Environment architecture for the autom8y ecosystem documents runtime config resolution, a 6-layer env loading model with SSM overlay, three cache mechanisms, and discovery logic across `a8-devenv.sh`, `ecosystem.conf`, `org-secrets.conf`, and encrypted secret files. The system uses direnv to load org-wide and project-specific environment variables with fallback caching, where SSM Parameter Store unconditionally overwrites Layer 2 (encrypted offline) values and downstream layers override earlier ones.

section_summaries.1-overview: This document covers four source files: `a8-devenv.sh` (1598-line core library symlinked to `~/.config/direnv/lib/a8.sh`), `ecosystem.conf` (identity/org config), `org-secrets.conf` (13-entry SSM manifest), and `env.defaults` (Layer 1 plaintext ecosystem defaults), excluding Terraform bridge and worktree trust mechanics.
section_summaries.2-system-architecture: The architecture chains six committed env file layers plus gitignored overrides through direnv, applies SSM overwrites after Layer 2, and maintains three filesystem caches (CodeArtifact token at 11h TTL, dotenvx key at 24h TTL, secrets at 24h TTL) that persist across sessions until expiry or cache miss triggers AWS API calls.
section_summaries.3-6-layer-loading-model: Six layers (ecosystem/project plaintext and encrypted defaults, per-env overrides, developer local) load via `set -a; source` or `_a8_source_encrypted` with dotenvx auto-detection, SSM exports unconditionally overwrite Layer 2 values, and Layer 2 acts as offline fallback when SSM is unreachable.
section_summaries.4-config-resolution: `_a8_resolve_config` discovers `ecosystem.conf` through four precedence levels (A8_ECOSYSTEM env var, CWD glob, upward walk to /, home config fallback) with idempotent session caching, requires five keys (`A8_ORG_NAME`, `A8_AWS_ACCOUNT`, `A8_AWS_REGION`, `A8_CODEARTIFACT_DOMAIN`, and optional `A8_ENV_CANONICAL`/`A8_SSM_PREFIX`), and derives eight internal variables.
section_summaries.5-execution-sequence: The 10-step sequence begins with auto-execution of `_a8_resolve_config` and `_a8_generate_functions`, then `use_a8` handler calls `_a8_trust_mise`, fetches dotenvx key from SSM or cache, loads Layers 1–5 plus legacy shim, invokes `_a8_load_org_secrets` for 3-pass SSM batch fetch, bridges Terraform vars, acquires CodeArtifact token, resolves tool tokens (GitHub), and activates virtual environments.
section_summaries.6-org-secrets-ssm: `_a8_load_org_secrets` fetches 13 org secrets from SSM paths under `${_A8_SSM_PREFIX}` in three batches (dotenvx key, main secrets, fallback), caches per-entry at `~/.cache/a8_secret_*` with 24h TTL, logs warnings on key absence, and unconditionally exports via `export VAR=val` with no skip-if-set guard.

autom8y::autom8y::literature-ai-first-sc.domain_summary: AI-first scheduling architecture synthesizes constraint satisfaction and event sourcing patterns with LLM-orchestrated agent design, focusing on multi-party calendar coordination that is fundamentally NP-hard and requires heuristic approaches. The literature spans mature foundational domains (constraint solvers, OAuth, CQRS event sourcing) alongside rapidly evolving AI-specific areas (LLM agents, MCP tool protocols, AI calendar products), with strong consensus that human-in-the-loop designs outperform fully autonomous agents in production deployments.

section_summaries.domain-calibration: This domain exhibits mixed evidence quality: computer science foundations and protocol specifications are well-documented with strong verification; AI-specific scheduling agent architecture relies heavily on 2024-2025 preprints and vendor documentation with limited peer-reviewed validation; production deployment patterns come from practitioner reports and single-case studies rather than large-scale comparative evaluations.
section_summaries.evidence-graded-findings: Foundational claims carry strong evidence (Google Calendar push notifications are explicitly unreliable; syncToken invalidation requires full re-sync; FreeBusy limited to 50 calendars per query); moderate evidence supports multi-agent graph architectures achieving 92% task completion and human-in-the-loop outperforming full automation; weak evidence supports JSON over Markdown for agent state and sub-agent isolation benefits.
section_summaries.executive-summary: Multi-party scheduling is NP-hard requiring heuristic or constraint-relaxation approaches; event sourcing with CQRS provides the strongest architectural foundation; human-in-the-loop designs outperform fully autonomous agents; key controversies exist around AI autonomy levels, SMS viability, and eventual consistency trade-offs.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Missing: quantitative benchmarks for FreeBusy API under load, large-scale multi-tenancy patterns for 1000+ concurrent users, comparative analysis of event store implementations (PostgreSQL vs specialized stores), MCP tool protocol safety guarantees, SMS compliance burden vs security payoff trade-offs, eventual consistency impact on user experience, and LLM-based scheduling performance on non-Latin scripts beyond Chinese.
section_summaries.methodology-note: Literature compiled through systematic searches of arXiv, ACM Digital Library, peer-reviewed conference proceedings (CHI, PACLIC, ICML), official vendor documentation (Google, Microsoft, Apple, Anthropic), and GitHub repositories; all claims independently verified against source material; evidence graded as STRONG (multiple independent corroboration), MODERATE (primary source with internal consistency), WEAK (single source or limited validation), or UNVERIFIED (claims without public evidence).
section_summaries.source-catalog: Forty-three sources spanning official documentation (Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, Apple), peer-reviewed papers (CHI 2017, PACLIC 2025, ACM conferences), preprints, and vendor documentation with verification status and relevance scores documented for each source.
section_summaries.thematic-synthesis: Five major themes emerge: AI agent architecture patterns (simple composable designs outperform complex frameworks; tool interface design equals prompt engineering effort); context management (agent quality depends more on context structure than model capability); calendar state management (event sourcing + CQRS with transactional guarantees for mutations); availability computation (FreeBusy query limitations, eventual consistency trade-offs); and human-AI task decomposition (automation allocation for microtasks vs human handling of edge cases).

autom8y::autom8y::literature-aws-step-fu.domain_summary: AWS Step Functions scatter-gather patterns for scheduled data aggregation leverage the Map state (Inline and Distributed modes) and Parallel state to implement fan-out/fan-in workloads with configurable partial failure tolerance. Production architectures require explicit error handling through Distributed Map's `ToleratedFailurePercentage`/`ToleratedFailureCount` or Catch-and-Pass patterns, bidirectional EventBridge integration for event-driven triggering and status publication, and decomposition from monolithic Lambda functions into single-responsibility state machines. The evidence base is anchored in Hohpe/Woolf's Enterprise Integration Patterns textbook and AWS's authoritative documentation, with sparse production case studies and under-documented cost modeling at scale. executive-summary | Scatter-Gather patterns in Step Functions converge on Map state (Inline/Distributed) and Parallel state for fan-out/fan-in, requiring explicit partial failure tolerance via `ToleratedFailurePercentage`/`ToleratedFailureCount` or Catch-and-Pass, with EventBridge providing bidirectional event-driven integration and migration from monolithic Lambdas well-documented by AWS. source-catalog | Literature base includes Hohpe/Woolf's Enterprise Integration Patterns (STRONG tier, textbook foundation), AWS official documentation (MODERATE tier, authoritative reference for Map/Parallel/error handling), AWS blog posts on EventBridge and workflow modularization (MODERATE tier), with limited production experience reports and sparse cost modeling evidence. thematic-synthesis | Synthesis identifies five key themes: Map state as primary scatter-gather mechanism with Distributed mode scaling to 10,000 concurrent executions; explicit partial failure tolerance through `ToleratedFailurePercentage`/`ToleratedFailureCount` or Catch handlers; Retry/Catch sequential evaluation with terminal errors (`States.Runtime`, `States.DataLimitExceeded`) non-retriable; bidirectional EventBridge integration via automatic Standard workflow event emission and EventBridge rule targeting; and parent-child decomposition patterns avoiding over-coupling and tight dependencies. evidence-graded-findings | Key findings with confidence grading: Distributed Map supports 10,000 concurrent child executions (STRONG), `ToleratedFailurePercentage` and `ToleratedFailureCount` threshold the acceptable failure band (STRONG), `ResultWriter` exports S3-based results avoiding 256 KiB payload limits (STRONG), `.waitForTaskToken` enables external readiness gating (STRONG), Standard workflows emit EventBridge events automatically on best-effort basis (STRONG), while `ItemBatcher` reduces cost for small-record workloads (MODERATE) and Retry at Distributed Map level re-runs all child executions (MODERATE). knowledge-gaps | Documentation lacks production cost modeling for Distributed Map at scale, sparse real-world failure scenario reports, limited guidance on optimal decomposition boundaries for parent-child workflows, absent comparative analysis of Inline Map vs. Distributed Map tradeoffs for specific item counts, and minimal documentation on EventBridge event ordering guarantees and latency characteristics. domain-calibration | The scatter-gather pattern in Step Functions is mature and well-documented for standard scenarios (Map state, error handling, EventBridge), with consensus on best practices (timeouts, S3 for large payloads, Distributed Map for scale); however, enterprise-scale cost optimization and production failure modes remain under-researched, limiting confidence in guidance for workloads exceeding 1,000 concurrent child executions. methodology-note | This literature review synthesized

autom8y::autom8y::literature-cost-consci.domain_summary: Cost-conscious coding treats cloud spend as a first-class engineering metric, with research demonstrating that individual lines of code can drive six- and seven-figure annual cost differences through mechanisms like excessive API calls, bloated logging, and mismatched compute models. The literature establishes ~50,000 daily invocations as the crossover point between Lambda and containers, with Lambda's hidden costs (NAT Gateway, CloudWatch, data transfer) often dominating raw compute charges, while peer-reviewed evidence on Python-specific optimization costs remains limited compared to practitioner reporting.

section_summaries.domain-calibration: The Green Software Foundation's SCI specification (ISO/IEC 21031:2024) provides the only standardized framework linking code efficiency to carbon intensity, creating alignment between cost optimization and sustainability goals and establishing carbon as a measurable outcome of cost-conscious engineering decisions.
section_summaries.evidence-graded-findings: Research demonstrates CloudWatch logging can cost 50x more than Lambda compute, debug logging cost $1.1M/year annualized, NAT Gateway and data transfer dominate Lambda bills (78% vs 22% compute), containers achieve 73% cost reduction over Lambda at scale, and package size dominates cold start performance (35MB = 13x slower than 1KB for JavaScript).
section_summaries.executive-summary: Cost-conscious coding is an emerging discipline treating cloud spend alongside availability and performance, with strong evidence for the 50,000 invocations/day Lambda-to-container crossover point and documented cases of individual code decisions causing six- and seven-figure annual cost overruns, though Python-specific optimization evidence remains largely practitioner-reported rather than peer-reviewed.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Peer-reviewed evidence is missing for Python-specific costs (Pydantic import overhead, structlog vs stdlib logging impact), dependency chain cold start impact quantification, real-world observability cost management strategies beyond anecdotal reports, and formal validation of the 50,000 invocations/day crossover threshold across different workload patterns.
section_summaries.methodology-note: Sources are graded on verification status (peer-reviewed, conference/vendor documentation, or blog posts with quantitative benchmarking), with confidence levels assigned to individual claims based on data quality, reproducibility, and source authority rather than citation count alone.
section_summaries.source-catalog: Seven verified sources and two unverified sources provide quantitative cost analysis across cloud compute models, infrastructure-as-code patterns, systems performance optimization, and Lambda runtime benchmarking, with peer-reviewed work (Feitosa et al. 2024) confirming only 1.3% of Terraform repositories show explicit cost awareness.
section_summaries.thematic-synthesis: Five core themes emerge: (1) hidden infrastructure costs dominate raw compute in Lambda, (2) observability infrastructure can consume 30% of cloud spend when unmanaged, (3) cost awareness remains absent from 98.7% of production IaC, (4) cold start performance is dominated by package size rather than memory allocation, and (5) compute model selection requires invocation volume thresholds rather than per-unit price comparison.

autom8y::autom8y::literature-library-ext.domain_summary: This knowledge domain synthesizes architectural patterns for building composable data pipelines, comparing theoretical foundations (the Dataflow Model, enterprise integration patterns) with practical implementations (Apache Beam, Dagster, Prefect, Luigi). It examines tradeoffs between task-centric, asset-centric, and dataflow-centric abstractions, debates on rule externalization versus embedding, and strategies for pipeline quality gates. source-catalog | Catalogs nine verified sources spanning foundational peer-reviewed research (Akidau et al. 2015 Dataflow Model, Hohpe & Woolf 2003 Enterprise Integration Patterns), official framework documentation (Apache Beam PTransform Style Guide, Dagster SDAs, Prefect v3 flows, Luigi design), and practitioner literature (Fowler on rules engines, Nected and InfoQ on implementation patterns). thematic-synthesis | Identifies four convergent patterns: Pipes and Filters for step decomposition, ParDo/GroupByKey as minimal sufficient primitives, builder-pattern configuration with immutability, and explicit dependency declaration; three divergent abstraction models (task-centric via Luigi/Prefect, asset-centric via Dagster, dataflow-centric via Beam); and three unresolved controversies around rule evaluation locus, readiness gate explicitness, and abstraction level appropriateness. evidence-graded-findings | Assigns strong consensus (5/5) to independent testability, configuration-driven composition, and uniform channel interfaces; moderate consensus (3-4/5) to asset-centric automation, decorator-based composition, and idempotency contracts; and weak consensus (2/5) to business-user rule authoring and dynamic pipeline composition without hardcoding. knowledge-gaps | Identifies missing formal treatment of error recovery in heterogeneous pipelines, limited guidance on cost-latency tradeoffs for fine-grained composition, insufficient academic study of readiness-gate strategies, and absence of comparative benchmarks across frameworks at production scale. domain-calibration | Rates evidence quality as moderate overall due to strong official documentation and practitioner literature but limited peer-reviewed research beyond the foundational Dataflow Model; notes that consensus exists on low-level primitives but diverges significantly on orchestration abstraction levels and deployment strategies. methodology-note | Declares this as an LLM-synthesized literature review requiring independent verification before production use; bases synthesis on official documentation from Apache Beam, Dagster, Prefect, and Luigi, foundational academic papers, and contemporary practitioner literature from martinfowler.com, InfoQ, and specialized engineering blogs. executive-summary | Provides the high-level overview stating consensus on Pipes and Filters, ParDo/GroupByKey, and declarative asset models, identifies key abstraction-level and rule-evaluation controversies, and notes moderate evidence quality driven by strong practitioner documentation but limited formal academic treatment.

autom8y::autom8y::literature-monorepo-di.domain_summary: This knowledge domain is a comprehensive literature review synthesizing academic papers, practitioner blogs, and vendor documentation on monorepo versus polyrepo architectures and their interoperability patterns. It establishes that repository topology is primarily an organizational decision, with dependency management and release cadence being more architecturally significant than code colocation, and documents the hybrid satellite pattern (monorepo for development + independent satellite repos for distribution) as an emerging best practice. source-catalog | Catalogs nine sources (2016-2024) spanning peer-reviewed papers (Google, Microsoft), vendor documentation (Nrwl, Azure SDK), and practitioner blogs, with relevance ratings 4-5 and verification status tracked. thematic-synthesis | Synthesizes sources into five core themes: (1) monorepo enables unified versioning and large-scale refactoring but requires custom tooling, (2) repository choice is organizational not purely technical, (3) hybrid patterns (monorepo + satellite repos via subtree-split) decouple development from distribution, (4) release cadence and shipping model matter more than repo topology, and (5) dependency management friction is the primary interop pain point. evidence-graded-findings | Identifies high-confidence findings (source-level dependency consumption in monorepos, diamond dependency resolution via lock-step upgrades, subtree-split enabling distributed satellites) and moderate-confidence findings (polyrepo tax claims, team cognition benefits, independent release cycles in monorepos) with supporting source citations and strength assessments. knowledge-gaps | Documents five key gaps: lack of formal empirical comparison of team velocity across repo topologies, minimal guidance on package registry design for satellite interop, limited literature on security/access control in hybrid models, sparse documentation of migration patterns from polyrepo to monorepo, and insufficient guidance on organizational structure alignment with technical boundaries. domain-calibration | Notes that literature is practitioner-dominated with limited peer-reviewed academic study; strongest consensus surrounds dependency management and shipping model impacts; claims about team cognition and polyrepo tax rest on weaker evidence; Azure SDK and Symfony case studies provide strongest real-world validation of satellite patterns. methodology-note | Explains that this review synthesizes openly available sources (no paywalled content fully analyzed); sources span 2016-2024 with continuous updates; verification status distinguishes between confirmed metadata/abstracts and full-text analysis; source type distribution includes 2 peer-reviewed papers, 4 vendor/community documentation sites, and 3 practitioner blogs. executive-summary | Establishes that neither pure monorepo nor polyrepo is universally superior; hybrid patterns combining central monorepo with independently deployable satellite repositories connected via package registries are increasingly documented in practice; dependency management friction and release coordination are more architecturally consequential than repository topology; strongest evidence supports Google's unified versioning model and the subtree-split distribution pattern used by Symfony and Azure SDK.

autom8y::autom8y::literature-service-orc.domain_summary: Service orchestration patterns for business logic migration decompose monolithic transactions into distributed service calls using sagas, with two coordination strategies (centralized orchestration vs. decentralized choreography) dominating the literature. The field emphasizes choosing orchestration for complex multi-step flows and choreography for simple event propagation, with durable execution platforms like Temporal representing current best practices for production reliability. This knowledge domain synthesizes peer-reviewed research, vendor documentation, and practitioner guides on saga patterns, distributed transaction trade-offs, and architectural decision frameworks.

autom8y::autom8y::literature-three-way-m.domain_summary: Three-way matching and variance detection in financial reconciliation encompasses multi-source transaction matching pipelines, standardized data models across payment processors (Stripe, Adyen), and ML-assisted anomaly detection with configurable tolerance thresholds. The domain synthesizes payment processor documentation, practitioner whitepapers, open-source engine implementations, and microservices architecture patterns to establish consensus on staged matching workflows (exact → fuzzy → ML → exception routing) and convergent schema designs for transaction identifiers, amount breakdowns, and journal type classifications.

section_summaries.domain-calibration: The domain demonstrates high maturity in data model standardization and matching pipeline architecture through payment processor documentation, moderate maturity in fuzzy matching algorithm application, and low maturity in formalized ML-assisted variance detection and exception workflow orchestration.
section_summaries.evidence-graded-findings: Strong evidence supports balance transaction models and journal-type taxonomies from official PSP documentation; moderate evidence supports three-way matching break categorization and fuzzy matching confidence bands; weak evidence supports dynamic ML-based threshold adjustment and semantic embedding algorithms in financial reconciliation production systems.
section_summaries.executive-summary: Multi-source reconciliation requires staged matching pipelines with configurable tolerance thresholds varying by transaction type, with strong consensus on a core data model convergent across Stripe and Adyen but mixed evidence quality on verdict taxonomies and ML-based dynamic threshold adjustment.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Open questions remain on optimal static vs. dynamic tolerance threshold strategies, formal standardization of verdict/anomaly taxonomies beyond practitioner consensus, production performance of semantic embedding vs. character-level algorithms, and cross-processor exception handling workflows in fully distributed payment ecosystems.
section_summaries.methodology-note: This synthesis integrates five official processor/vendor documentation sources (strong primary evidence), two technical whitepapers/blogs (moderate secondary evidence), one open-source reference implementation (weak architectural guidance), and prioritizes consensus claims graded strong where multiple independent sources converge on identical technical patterns.
section_summaries.source-catalog: Six primary sources (Stripe/Adyen official documentation, Luxoft whitepaper, OpenRec engine, Optimus guides, Mercari engineering blog) provide payment processor data models, architectural patterns, and fuzzy matching algorithms with strong relevance ratings.
section_summaries.thematic-synthesis: Key themes include staged matching pipelines (exact/fuzzy/ML/exception), convergent schema design across PSPs (balance transaction model), three-tier fee decomposition (interchange/scheme/markup), field mapping normalization challenges in multi-PSP environments, four-tier confidence thresholds (95-100/85-94/70-84/below-70), and microservices consistency patterns across distributed ledger reconciliation.

autom8y::autom8y::narrative-observabilit.domain_summary: Narrative observability is an emerging observability category combining AI-powered trace analysis with business-context story generation to translate infrastructure events into narratives that non-technical stakeholders understand. The market is undergoing consolidation around AI assistant commoditization (all major vendors shipped NL-to-query tooling by 2025), creating whitespace for domain-specific business narrative generation—a gap autom8y's devconsole plugin architecture with LensProtocol and domain-aware SpanProvider is positioned to address.

section_summaries.competitive-matrix: All major observability vendors now offer NL-to-query AI assistants (Honeycomb May 2023, New Relic July 2025, Grafana October 2025); anomaly detection algorithms range from SARIMA-based (Datadog) to deterministic causal AI (Dynatrace) to statistical-LLM hybrid (GALA); no vendor combines domain-specific business context with trace narratives, leaving infrastructure-to-business-impact translation unaddressed.
section_summaries.evidence-grades: Grade A sources are official product documentation and API references; Grade B sources are vendor engineering blogs, conference talks, and corroborated press releases; Grade C sources are analyst reports and community posts; Grade D sources are user reviews; only shipped features and peer-reviewed research included, roadmap items and pre-2022 sources excluded.
section_summaries.executive-summary: The observability market (USD 3.35B in 2026, projected USD 6.93B by 2031) is consolidating around AI-assisted querying as table stakes while diverging into domain-specific narrative generation; autom8y's
section_summaries.findings: Major vendors (Honeycomb, Datadog, New Relic, Elastic, Grafana, Splunk, Dynatrace) have deployed AI assistants for natural language querying using GPT-3.5-turbo and semantic embeddings, making this capability table stakes; Honeycomb's BubbleUp (comparative histogram analysis) and Datadog Watchdog (SARIMA-based anomaly detection with service topology RCA) represent state-of-the-art infrastructure-level explanation, while incident management platforms (incident.io, FireHydrant) lead in narrative generation for postmortems; academic research (GALA, MicroRCA-Agent) demonstrates graph-augmented LLM reasoning improves RCA accuracy by 42% but remains infrastructure-centric.
section_summaries.implications-for-autom8y: autom8y's devconsole plugin architecture occupies genuine whitespace by combining LensProtocol domain awareness with SpanProvider integration to generate business-context narratives ("booking failed because calendar sync exceeded SMS window") rather than infrastructure narratives; the plugin model with entry-point discovery enables vertical/domain-specific observability differentiation as the market shifts beyond commoditized AI assistants.
section_summaries.methodology: 42 sources evaluated across product documentation (A-grade), engineering blogs (B-grade), analyst reports (C-grade), and user reviews (D-grade), with inclusion criteria requiring shipped capabilities post-2022 and exclusion of roadmap-only announcements.
section_summaries.source-registry: Primary sources include Honeycomb official docs and engineering blogs [1-10], Datadog APM and Watchdog documentation [11-18], Lightstep Change Intelligence docs pre-EOL [19-22], vendor AI announcements 2023-2025 [23-28], and academic papers from arXiv and EuroSys '24 [29-33].

autom8y::autom8y::observability-posture.domain_summary: Autom8y observability posture audit conducted post-ADOT remediation, achieving 81% overall maturity across ten dimensions covering trace instrumentation, alerting, logging, dashboards, and SLO recording. The assessment reveals deep instrumentation for ECS services (72% trace depth), 78 provisioned alert rules with 13 currently firing (82%), and strong log-trace correlation (95%), but identifies gaps in Lambda force_flush handling, deployment.environment propagation to 4 of 9 services, and shallow Lambda span hierarchy.

section_summaries.adot-collector-cloudwatch-alarms: ADOT collector resolved post-remediation with 2/2 ECS tasks healthy, all 30 CloudWatch alarms in OK state, data service sidecar loop fixed, achieving 90% maturity.
section_summaries.alert-rules-live-state: 78 Grafana alert rules across SLO burn rates, saturation, and custom domain logic; 13 currently firing including real issues (auth 5xx, data latency burns, materializer silent failures) and suspect trace pipeline DMS alerts that may reflect TraceQL query resolution gaps rather than delivery failures.
section_summaries.dashboard-coverage: 25 custom dashboards with good breadth across services and Four Golden Signals; Lambda services underrepresented with coverage gaps at 82% maturity.
section_summaries.deployment-environment-gap: 5 of 9 services (auth, ads, data, auth-mysql-sync, sms) missing ENVIRONMENT variable propagation for resource identification; 55% maturity due to incomplete attribute coverage across service portfolio.
section_summaries.executive-summary: Overall platform maturity is 81% with ADOT collector healthy post-remediation; trace pipeline runbooks deployed; remaining 13 firing alerts reflect genuine service health issues (auth availability, data latency burns, materializer failures) rather than infrastructure gaps.
section_summaries.log-trace-correlation: End-to-end correlation verified: Loki logs contain real trace_ids that successfully resolve in Tempo with matching spans, achieving 95% correlation maturity.
section_summaries.runbook-coverage: 38 runbooks on disk with 100% of 78 alert rules mapped to resolvable runbook_url; all alert rules have documented remediation paths achieving 95% coverage.
section_summaries.satellite-telemetry-status: All 4 satellites (auth, data, asana, ads) depend on autom8y-telemetry package; asana includes [aws] extra for Lambda instru
section_summaries.slosli-recording-rules: 64 SLI recording rules across 4 ECS services (auth, data, asana, ads) tracking availability and latency; Lambda services have zero recording rules, creating asymmetry in SLO visibility at 85% maturity.
section_summaries.trace-instrumentation-depth: ECS services fully instrumented with SERVER/CLIENT/DB/cache spans via FastAPIInstrumentor and autom8y-http; Lambda services use shallow @instrument_lambda decorator (one root span) with SMS client-lead missing force_flush() causing potential data loss; 3-4 Lambda services not inspected.
section_summaries.trace-pipeline-alerts-phase-c: Dead-man-switch rules deployed with 30m for_duration for both auth heartbeat and all-service ingestion; both currently firing despite ADOT health and verified trace flow in Loki/Tempo, indicating possible Grafana Cloud Tempo metrics-generator configuration issue with TraceQL count_over_time() queries.

autom8y::autom8y::otel-instrumentation-c.domain_summary: OpenTelemetry semantic conventions define standardized attributes across 20+ technology domains with a five-level maturity lifecycle; only HTTP and core database conventions are stable as of v1.40.0, while GenAI conventions (gen_ai.* namespace) remain at Development maturity. OTel Weaver enables schema-first tooling with custom registries using reverse-domain prefixes (e.g., com.autom8y.*), and vendor adoption is strong across Datadog, Elastic, and AWS with mapping to proprietary schemas. This knowledge domain guides autom8y-telemetry SDK design for scheduling/booking domain-specific observability using federated convention registries and test-trace correlation patterns.

section_summaries.custom-convention-extension-patterns: Organizations must prefix custom attributes with reverse domain names (com.acme.shopname) avoiding existing OTel namespaces; Datadog maps OTel to proprietary schema with span.type inference, Elastic converged ECS into OTel post-April-2023 donation, and AWS X-Ray distinguishes annotations (indexed) from metadata (non-indexed).
section_summaries.executive-summary: GenAI semantic conventions (gen_ai.*) introduced in v1.26.0 define comprehensive LLM inference, embeddings, tool execution, and agent attributes; OTel Weaver supports custom registries with reverse-domain prefixes for organizational extensions following a federated model endorsed by the 2025 stability proposal.
section_summaries.genai-semantic-conventions: GenAI conventions from Issue #327 (September 2023) define operation identity, request config, response metadata, token usage, tool execution, and conversation attributes with well-known provider/operation names; Datadog natively supports gen_ai.* v1.37+ with automatic mapping to its schema, and an August 2025 agentic systems extension proposal adds tasks/actions/agents/teams/artifacts/memory domains.
section_summaries.implications-for-autom8y: autom8y-telemetry SDK should define com.autom8y.* or organization-scoped namespace for scheduling/booking/reconciliation attributes using Weaver for validation and code-generation; implement opt-in content capture for PII-sensitive data following gen_ai.* pattern; use InMemorySpanExporter with pytest-otel or pytest-opentelemetry for test-trace correlation closing observability-by-design to test-driven development loop.
section_summaries.methodology: Search strategy evaluated 34 sources across OpenTelemetry official documentation, GitHub repositories, vendor documentation, and community guides, with inclusion criteria focused on convention design, extension patterns, and instrumentation architecture from 2022 onward.
section_summaries.otel-semantic-conventions-architecture: OpenTelemetry v1.40.0 organizes 20+ domains (HTTP stable, Database stable, GenAI/Messaging/RPC/FaaS experimental) using dot-notation naming rules (lowercase, snake_case, singular for entities, plural for arrays) with five-level maturity progression where stable groups cannot regress and must use opt-in patterns for unstable attributes.

autom8y::autom8y::release/dependency-top.domain_summary: This knowledge domain documents the complete dependency topology, release sequencing strategy, and impact analysis for the autom8y SDK ecosystem across 12 core packages and 4 satellite consumers, enabling safe parallel deployments and identifying critical breaking-change risks.

section_summaries.blast-radius: autom8y-config carries CRITICAL severity impacting 8 downstream consumers (4 direct SDKs + 4 satellites), autom8y-log and autom8y-http carry HIGH severity across 8 consumers, while autom8y-events carries NONE with zero active consumers.
section_summaries.cross-sdk-dependency-dag: autom8y-config serves as the root foundation with transitive dependencies flowing through autom8y-log to autom8y-telemetry, autom8y-ai, and autom8y-meta, while autom8y-core branches independently to autom8y-auth with optional telemetry/log dependencies, and autom8y-http multiplexes to autom8y-stripe, autom8y-ai, autom8y-meta, and autom8y-auth.
section_summaries.satellite-consumer-map-verified-2026-03-: Four satellite consumers (autom8y-ads, autom8y-asana, autom8y-data, autom8y-sms) depend on 5–7 production SDKs each with optional auth/telemetry dependencies, verified 2026-03-07.
section_summaries.satellite-inter-dependencies: All four satellites are leaf nodes with no inter-dependencies, permitting safe parallel execution across all satellite deployments.
section_summaries.validated-publish-order-4-phase: A 4-phase sequential publish strategy enforces autom8y-config → autom8y-log → {autom8y-telemetry, autom8y-ai, autom8y-meta in parallel} → autom8y-auth to guarantee all transitive dependencies resolve correctly.
section_summaries.version-drift-notes: autom8y-sms pins autom8y-ai at >=0.1.0 despite published 1.1.0 (major drift requiring tightening), while auth and meta show only patch-level drift across ads/asana/data consumers.

autom8y::autom8y::release/history.domain_summary: This knowledge domain documents the autom8y platform's release history from March 3-9, 2026, tracking SDK deployments, service updates, and infrastructure changes across a microservices ecosystem with ECS, Lambda, and Terraform components. RELEASE-LOG | Contains 6 chronological release entries (March 3-9, 2026) documenting SDK version bumps, service deployments, infrastructure changes, test outcomes, and carry-forward issues across autom8y's satellite services (ads, data, sms, asana), observability stack, and core platform services.

autom8y::autom8y::release/platform-profi.domain_summary: The autom8y platform comprises a monorepo workspace with 13 Python SDK packages distributed via AWS CodeArtifact and 6 core services (pull-payments, reconcile-ads, reconcile-spend, slack-alert, auth-mysql-sync, sms-performance-report) deployed as ECS or Lambda workloads. The release automation orchestrates SDK publishing and multi-satellite service deployments through GitHub Actions with 4-stage sequential chains and cross-repo dispatch coordination. repository-ecosystem-map | 13 Python SDK packages managed via uv with CodeArtifact distribution; 6 services consuming subsets of core SDKs (autom8y-config, autom8y-log, autom8y-core, autom8y-http, autom8y-stripe, autom8y-telemetry) with pull-payments as the highest-dependency consumer. pipeline-chain-discovery | Four-stage sdk-publish-v2.yml chain (detect-changes → test with ruff/mypy/pytest → version-check gate → uv build/publish) triggered by main branch push or force_publish dispatch; service deployments routed through dispatch.yml or satellite-receiver.yml that invoke service-deploy.yml/.yml as workflow_call jobs within receiver run context. configuration-artifacts | Root pyproject.toml with all SDKs as workspace members using uv_build>=0.7,<1; CodeArtifact domain=autom8y/repository=autom8y-python; publish via `just ca-publish {sdk-name}`; reconcile-ads and sms-performance-report missing from services.yaml manifest despite having implementation code. known-issues | autom8y-sms pins autom8y-ai>=0.1.0 versus published 1.1.0 (loose floor across major boundary requiring tightening); autom8y-events 0.1.0 published but has zero satellite consumers.

autom8y::autom8y::scar-tissue.domain_summary: # Codebase Scar Tissue This document catalogs 60+ production failures and security incidents across the autom8y Python monorepo (services, SDKs, Lambda functions, Terraform IaC) spanning auth service outages, privilege escalation vulnerabilities, observability blackholes, and test isolation breakage. It maps failures to root causes, fix locations, and defensive patterns implemented to prevent recurrence.

section_summaries.category-coverage: Security/auth bypass and config drift/environment variable mismatch dominate failure distribution (7 and 15+ scars respectively), with integration failure (12), observability/tracing (9), CI/CD (7), and test isolation (5) forming secondary clusters; race conditions, message queue failures, and cache invalidation bugs remain undetected.
section_summaries.defensive-pattern-documentation: Auth implemented constant-time dummy hash verification (SCAR-012), token_prefix indexing for O(1) reset lookup (SCAR-014), non-overridable ClassVar JWT algorithm (SCAR-023), dual business_id+token_hash refresh validation (SCAR-013), and 1300-line regression test suite for CRITICAL-001-004 security scars; observability implemented force-flush in Lambda try/finally blocks and traceparent header injection; test isolation implemented cache_clear() autouse fixtures and --import-mode=importlib pytest configuration.
section_summaries.failure-catalog: Auth service experienced 11-issue 503 outage (timezone schema mismatches, secrets misconfiguration, missing dependencies, silent deployment failures) plus 7 security hardening scars (timing attacks, token bypass, DoS, JWT algorithm override, audit log mutation) across 26 total critical fixes in auth; additionally identified 9 observability scars (Lambda span loss, missing FastAPI instrumentation, OTel endpoint misconfiguration), 5 test isolation failures (singleton state leakage, pytest-asyncio API breaks, import path collisions), 7 CI/CD deployment failures (missing force-new-deployment flag, ECS health check timeouts), and 6 data integrity issues in reconcile-spend service.
section_summaries.fix-location-mapping: Critical security fixes concentrated in `services/auth/src/` (token verification, rate limiting, config hardening) and `sdks/python/autom8y-config/` (environment variable resolution); deployment fixes in `.github/workflows/service-deploy.yml` and `.github/workflows/sdk-publish-v2.yml`; observability fixes in `sdks/python/autom8y-telemetry/` (Lambda processor, FastAPI instrumentation, OTel endpoint handling).

autom8y::autom8y::security-posture.domain_summary: The autom8y platform implements a comprehensive post-campaign security posture governed by ADR-SEC-GATE-POLICY, featuring a SARIF 2.1.0-based pipeline with ten active producers (Gitleaks, Trivy, Checkov, CodeQL, Semgrep, zizmor, and cross-repo tools) and a severity-based gate policy engine that blocks CRITICAL findings and CISA KEV matches unconditionally while using EPSS scoring (threshold 0.1) to differentiate HIGH severity responses. The architecture enforces five SEC-* invariants including mandatory expiry for exemptions, achieves ~80% noise reduction through EPSS filtering, and operates at near-zero cost ($2-32/month) using open-source tools and GitHub-included services.

section_summaries.cost-model: Operating at $0-32/month ($0-648/year budget) using entirely open-source SARIF tools (Gitleaks, Trivy, govulncheck, zizmor, Semgrep OSS), GitHub-included CodeQL, free public APIs (EPSS, KEV), and optional GuardDuty ECS (~$2-32/month).
section_summaries.cross-references: Security decisions documented in ADR-SEC-GATE-POLICY (CONSTITUTIONAL), SARIF-PIPELINE-ARCHITECTURE spec, design constraints (TENSION-P014, LOAD-P009, RISK-P007/P008), scar tissue documentation (CRITICAL-001–004, SEC-FP-* patterns), and campaign retrospective.
section_summaries.gate-policy-engine: The severity-to-action matrix enforces unconditional CRITICAL blocks, CISA KEV override to CRITICAL, EPSS >0.1 threshold for HIGH blocking with 80% noise reduction, and 7-30 day SLAs, with free API enrichment from FIRST.org and CISA KEV feeds and fail-safe CVSS-only fallback.
section_summaries.runtime-monitoring: Active monitoring includes GuardDuty ECS for container runtime threats (Terraform-created, pending apply), harden-runner in audit mode on releases and service builds, Schemathesis API fuzzing limited to auth service (max_examples=200), and CodeRabbit AI code review (app install pending).
section_summaries.sarif-pipeline-architecture: Ten SARIF producers across autom8y and a8 repositories output SARIF 2.1.0 via GitHub Actions (Gitleaks, Trivy Container/IaC, Checkov, CodeQL Python/Go, Semgrep, zizmor, govulncheck) with supplemental non-SARIF tools (TruffleHog weekly, dependency-review-action, pip-audit-gate.py) all uploading conditionally to public repos only.
section_summaries.sec-invariants: Five SEC-* invariants mandate SARIF output (10 active producers), gate policy consistency (ADR-governed), in-repo config portability, no permanent exemptions with maximum 30d/90d/CRITICAL expiry rules, and documented scar annotations (30+ SEC-FP-* rationales) with SNR >3:1 trial graduation within 8 sprints.

autom8y::autom8y::test-coverage.domain_summary: This knowledge domain documents test coverage metrics, testing patterns, and infrastructure across the autom8y Python codebase, comprising 21 SDK packages and 13 services with approximately 0.60 test-to-source file ratio. It identifies critical untested paths in error handling and financial models while establishing conventions for async testing, mocking strategies (respx/unittest.mock/moto), and advanced patterns including adversarial and property-based testing.

section_summaries.coverage-gaps: SDK packages show significant variance with autom8y-telemetry and autom8y-log well-covered (>100% test ratio) but autom8y-stripe, autom8y-core, and autom8y-events critically undercovered (<50% ratio with untested error paths and financial data handling).
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Actual branch coverage percentages remain unknown without running test suite; autom8y-meta test content, CI gating enforcement, and tools/ecosystem-observer test coverage are undocumented.
section_summaries.test-structure-summary: 479 test files target ~779 source files across SDKs (0.62 ratio) and services (0.60 ratio), with integration tests structurally separated via `tests/integration/` directories and `pytest.mark.integration` markers, and optional coverage enforcement configured via `--cov-fail-under=80` in satellite-ci-reusable.yml.
section_summaries.testing-conventions: Tests use class-based structure with `async def test_{action}_{condition}` methods, `asyncio_mode = "auto"` for async support, pure pytest assertions, and three mocking strategies: respx for httpx, unittest.mock for general mocking, and moto for AWS service mocking.

autom8y::knossos::architecture.domain_summary: Knossos is a Go module providing two subsystems (ari CLI workflow harness and Clew Slack bot) as a single binary, with embedded FSes for rites, templates, hooks, agents, and mena files enabling standalone distribution. The architecture separates concerns across four layers (CLI surface, command wiring, domain logic, infrastructure) with documented import invariants preventing circular dependencies and maintaining testability.

section_summaries.data-flow: `ari sync` triggers `Materializer` (resolves rite via resolution chain, transforms via compiler, outputs to channel dirs, tracks provenance); `ari serve` wires HTTP server with `SlackHandler` (dedup→thread context→triage→reason pipeline) dispatching to `reason.Pipeline` (intent classify→BM25/embedding retrieve→trust assess→context assemble→Claude respond); `ari ask` queries `SearchIndex` (BM25+RRF over commands/concepts/rites/agents/routing) persisting to container `/data/knowledge-index` with runtime path resolved in serve startup.
section_summaries.entry-points-and-api-surface: `cmd/ari/main.go` is the single entry point that injects build-time version info, registers five embedded FSes via `common.SetEmbeddedAssets`, and delegates to `root.Execute()` which registers 35+ Cobra subcommands.
section_summaries.key-abstractions: Core abstractions include `Materializer` (source→transform→channel pipeline), `SearchIndex` with BM25+RRF fusion, `KnowledgeIndex` (BM25+embedding+graph+summary for Clew), `session.FSM` (NONE→ACTIVE→PARKED→ARCHIVED), `reason.Pipeline` (query classify→retrieve→assess→generate), `Trust.Scorer` (freshness×retrieval×coverage geometric mean), and `SlackHandler` with event dedup and thread-context TTL eviction.
section_summaries.layer-boundaries: Four enforced layers stack vertically: CLI Surface (Cobra commands), Command Wiring (`BaseContext`, `SessionContext`, asset refs), Domain Logic (session/rite/materialize/reason/slack/serve), and Infrastructure/Leaf (errors/output/paths/registry/resolution), with explicit import invariants documented in source (e.g., reason/ does not import serve/ or cmd/, trust/search do not import reason/).
section_summaries.package-structure: The codebase organizes into `cmd/ari/` (binary entry), `internal/cmd/*` (CLI surface with 25+ command packages), and `internal/*` (domain logic and leaf packages), with hub packages like `internal/cmd/serve`, `internal/cmd/root`, and `internal/materialize` importing many siblings, and leaf packages like `internal/registry`, `internal/resolution`, and `internal/mena` having zero internal imports.

autom8y::knossos::conventions.domain_summary: Knossos codebase conventions define structured error handling, file organization patterns, and domain-specific idioms for a large Go project (704 files, 46 packages) with strict CLI/domain separation. This reference enforces consistency across the `cmd/` and `internal/` layers through typed errors, concern-per-file organization, and domain state patterns.

section_summaries.domain-specific-idioms: Core patterns include typed string constants with `IsValid()` methods for domain states, `Options` structs paired with `WithOptions()` methods, `Result` structs for operation outcomes, `Resolver` services for contextual lookup, `Tabular`/`Textable` interfaces for output, `SchemaVersion` fields in persisted types, and a LEAF package convention preventing circular dependencies.
section_summaries.error-handling-style: The project uses a dual-layer system: `internal/errors` provides structured, JSON-serializable domain errors with typed codes (SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE) and `Is*` predicates for boundaries, while `fmt.Errorf` wraps errors in internal packages; errors propagate upward via `errors.Wrap()` with cause tracking, and the CLI boundary uses `errors.Handled()` sentinel to prevent double-printing.
section_summaries.file-organization: Code splits between `cmd/ari/` (minimal entry point) and `internal/cmd/` (command implementations) with domain logic isolated in `internal/` across 46 packages using a concern-per-file pattern (`types.go`, `validate.go`, `manifest.go`), while large packages subdivide into subdirectories by sub-concern (e.g., `materialize/compiler/`, `search/bm25/`).
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Hook wiring (`internal/hook`, 34 files), LLM reasoning pipeline error propagation (`internal/reason`), store-level error handling in BM25/embedding search (`internal/search`), and procession package patterns remain incompletely sampled.
section_summaries.naming-patterns: Package names are singular lowercase nouns, types follow `*Options`/`*Result`/`*Resolver` conventions, domain state values are typed constants (`StatusActive`), error codes are `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`, constructors use `New*` prefix with domain errors using `Err*`, and acronyms remain all-caps (`SessionID`, `JSON`).

autom8y::knossos::design-constraints.domain_summary: Knossos is a codebase for managing AI agent orchestration with Claude/Gemini, featuring provenance tracking, materialization pipelines, and behavioral contracts across multiple harnesses. The design embeds significant technical debt captured in 17 tensions, 8 trade-offs, and load-bearing code patterns that prevent naive refactoring without cascading failures. ABSTRACTION-GAP-MAPPING | Hardcoded `.claude/` paths block Gemini parity, duplicate `MaxTurns` fields exist across frontmatter and BehavioralContract, deprecated `RiteManifest.Skills/.Commands` fields persist, and tier traversal logic duplicates across `materialize/source` and `resolution` packages. LOAD-BEARING-CODE-IDENTIFICATION | Critical fragile patterns include `WriteIfChanged()` preventing CC file watcher crashes, three-pass rewrite ordering in content rewrites, dual-format event readers, append-only type rename maps, satellite region preservation in inscription mergers, and hardcoded `PreToolUse` hook wire format as a security boundary. EVOLUTION-CONSTRAINT-DOCUMENTATION | Changes range from SAFE (local-only like `internal/resolution/chain.go`) to COORDINATED (multi-file like adding a TargetChannel across 5 files) to MIGRATION (breaking changes requiring migration functions) to FROZEN (untouchable like `PreToolUse` wire name and `.claude/` directory). RISK-ZONE-MAPPING | Non-transactional materialize pipeline allows partial failures without rollback, `sync.Once` KnossosHome singleton risks test contamination, hardcoded channel dirs block multi-harness support, and 5-minute lock timeouts lack heartbeat mechanisms. KNOWLEDGE-GAPS | Unmapped constraints include debt triage heuristics in `naxos/triage.go`, white sails signaling thresholds, session FSM transition invariants, template schema validation, JSON schema versioning, `hooks.yaml` end-to-end format, rite registration constraints, and MCP ownership model. TENSION-CATALOG-COMPLETENESS | 17 tensions documented with 16 accurate and 1 resolved (TENSION-015): key unresolved tensions include dual `OwnerType` definitions, hardcoded `.claude/` paths, `channelDirOverride` mutation patterns, string sync without shared constants, and split output targets (settings to channel dir, MCP to project root). TRADE-OFF-DOCUMENTATION | Eight documented trade-offs justify current design: `WriteIfChanged()` avoids file watcher crashes, dual-format event readers persist until session archival, provenance in `.knossos/` despite context exposure risk, CC event names as canonical vocabulary, XDG path duplication due to import cycles, two-manifest architecture for sync safety, and `channelDirOverride` save-restore as pragmatic hack.

autom8y::knossos::feat/agent-scaffolding.domain_summary: Agent scaffolding framework for autom8y/knossos that enforces structural correctness and lifecycle management of Claude agents through archetypes, validation tiers, and ownership models. Provides three guarantees: authoring scaffolding from archetypes, structural governance via WARN/STRICT validation, and summonable agent lifecycle management in ~/.claude/agents/. Three-ownership section model (platform/author/derived) with tier field (standing/rite/summonable) controlling materialization independent of capabilities. PURPOSE-AND-DESIGN-RATIONALE | Three archetypes (orchestrator, specialist, reviewer) with corresponding tool constraints and turn limits, three materialization tiers, three-phase validation pipeline (parse/JSON Schema/semantic), and section ownership model (platform re-rendered, author preserved, derived generated from frontmatter). CONCEPTUAL-MODEL | 13 type values map to 3 archetypes; standing agents always materialized, rite agents per-sync, summonable agents on-demand; two-tier validation enforces frontmatter + archetype rules; section ownership determines preservation vs regeneration strategy. IMPLEMENTATION-MAP | internal/agent/ provides core logic (frontmatter validation, archetype definitions, Go template scaffolding, section parsing, platform section regeneration, validation tiers, MCP validation) while internal/cmd/agent/ implements CLI commands (new, validate, update, summon with collision checking, dismiss, roster, list, embody) using 7-step agent_transform.go pipeline for materialization. BOUNDARIES-AND-FAILURE-MODES | Standing agents without files silently lack descriptions; MaxTurns not exposed as CC constraint; summon collision checker degrades gracefully on uninitialized projects; non-standard types silently default to specialist; template placeholder extraction fragile; provenance checks non-fatal; ADR-0024 missing from disk. KNOWLEDGE-GAPS | JSON Schema content not examined; archetype .md.tpl template content not read; internal/perspective package tracing incomplete; skill policy application details not reviewed.

autom8y::knossos::feat/artifact-registry.domain_summary: **Domain Summary:** The Work Artifact Registry (.ledge/) is a two-tier document provenance system that tracks non-code work products through session-local registries (artifacts.yaml) and a derived project registry (registry.yaml), with graduated promotion paths gated by sails status and triggered during ari session wrap. The system manages eight artifact types across a four-state lifecycle (REGISTERED → Graduated → Shelf) with phase inference, multi-dimensional querying, and comprehensive provenance tracking via frontmatter injection.

section_summaries.boundaries-and-failure-modes: Non-fatal graduation during wrap, REVIEW/SPIKE filename detection gap in detectArtifact prefix map, destination-exists blocks promote (no overwrite), non-atomic promotion (write-then-remove), hardcoded project registry path (.claude/), AggregateAll lacking physical file restoration, and filename collision overwrites across sessions.
section_summaries.conceptual-model: Four-state lifecycle (REGISTERED → Graduated → Shelf), eight artifact types (PRD/TDD/TestPlan/Runbook → specs; ADR → decisions; Review → reviews; Spike → spikes; Code → immutable), phase inference mapping, provenance layering (graduated_at/original_path and promoted_at/promoted_from), and project indexing by Phase/Type/Specialist/Session.
section_summaries.implementation-map: Five artifact package files (registry.go for CRUD, aggregate.go for session-to-project aggregation, query.go for AND filtering, graduate.go for copy-with-frontmatter) plus two ledge files (promote.go and auto_promote.go) with CLI commands artifact (register/query/list/rebuild) and ledge (list/promote).
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Three unknowns: ari ledge query subcommand not found, hook integration for auto-registration not found, --auto-promote default behavior unconfirmed.
section_summaries.purpose-and-design-rationale: Two-tier provenance tracking separates session registries (artifacts.yaml, eagerly written) from project registry (registry.yaml, derived aggregate) with graduated and shelf paths, white-sails-gated auto-promotion, code artifact immutability, and non-fatal graduation triggered by ari session wrap.

autom8y::knossos::feat/clew-handoff-prot.domain_summary: The Orchestrator Handoff Protocol enables formal work transfer between specialist agents (requirements-analyst → architect → principal-engineer → qa-adversary → orchestrator) within autom8y sessions, with externalized routing for session log reconstruction and artifact integrity validation at phase boundaries via a two-step prepare/execute mechanism. The protocol uses hardcoded workflow topology, event pair emissions (task_end/task_start), v2 event format, and lightweight regex-based throughline extraction for session context reconstruction delegated to Moirai.

section_summaries.boundaries-and-failure-modes: Hardcoded 5-agent registry decoupled from rite manifest, sequence enforcement in prepare but not execute, non-blocking event write failures, regex throughline extraction vulnerable to substring false positives, and distinct procession-level handoff schema with separate tooling.
section_summaries.conceptual-model: Hardcoded five-agent directed topology enforces handoff sequence validation, event pairs written in v2 format with phase transition tracking, and lightweight regex throughline extraction handles log reconstruction.
section_summaries.implementation-map: Six command files in internal/cmd/handoff/ implement agent/sequence validation (prepare.go, 344 lines), artifact validation and dry-run (execute.go, 215 lines), and event log scanning (status.go, history.go), with HandoffValidator in internal/validation/handoff.go currently incomplete for file path artifact validation.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: ADR-0008 and ADR-0012 unavailable on disk, procession handoff Go validation type unexamined, and v3 TypedEvent migration timeline undefined.
section_summaries.purpose-and-design-rationale: Two-step prepare/execute design validates artifacts and emits task_end/task_start events, with SESSION_CONTEXT.md mutations delegated to Moirai rather than inline execution.

autom8y::knossos::feat/embedded-assets.domain_summary: Single-binary embedded asset distribution solves bootstrapping by embedding KNOSSOS runtime assets (rites, mena, agents, hooks, processions) directly in the binary using //go:embed directives, enabling zero external dependencies at startup with version coherence between binary and assets. The hybrid model serves embedded assets as lowest-priority fallback tier in resolution chains while extracting mena and agents to XDG_CONFIG_HOME during ari init with .ari-version sentinel for upgrade detection. PURPOSE: Embedded assets bootstrap users immediately after download with zero external dependencies and automatic version coherence between binary and assets via .ari-version sentinel-triggered wipe-and-reextract on mismatch. CONCEPTUAL: Four embedded asset roles: EmbeddedRites as fallback tier via fs.Sub sub-FS, EmbeddedMena/EmbeddedAgents extracted to XDG during init, EmbeddedHooksYAML write-once bootstrap if missing, EmbeddedProcessions for template resolution, with global state flow from embed.go through SetEmbedded* in main.go to internal/assets store. IMPLEMENTATION: Six //go:embed exports in module-root embed.go feed into internal/assets package-level storage, passed via main.go SetEmbedded* to Materializer WithEmbeddedFS methods in resolver.go and materialize.go, with ari init extracting mena via extractEmbeddedMenaToXDG in initialize/init.go. BOUNDARIES: embed.go must reside at module root, SourceEmbedded skips platform mena injection, EmbeddedHooksYAML lacks version-aware upgrade, rite.Discovery excludes embedded FS, missing SetEmbedded calls silently disable embedded tier, fs.Sub failure falls back to os.DirFS, XDG RemoveAll failures retain stale mena with warning. GAPS: TDD-single-binary-completion.md reference unresolved, EmbeddedAgents user-scope consumption path untraceable, RiteChain constructor signature inferred from callsites only.
