autom8y::knossos::literature-formal-veri.domain_summary: Formal verification of consensus protocols combines model checking (TLA+, SAT/SMT) for automation with interactive theorem proving (Coq, Isabelle/HOL, Dafny) for unbounded systems, and decidable verification (EPR) as a middle ground. Industrial practice at AWS demonstrates TLA+ catches subtle bugs missed by testing, while end-to-end verified implementations like Verdi and IronFleet achieve comparable performance to unverified systems with 3-4 person-years of effort. The field has matured from academic pursuit to production-relevant practice with strong consensus on formal methods' bug-finding capability, though cost-effectiveness of full implementation verification versus specification-only approaches remains debated.

section_summaries.domain-calibration: This domain represents mature, academically grounded practice (primarily PLDI, OOPSLA, SOSP venues) with strong industrial validation (AWS production use since 2011), though practical adoption remains limited outside systems requiring extreme reliability; fringe areas include automated invariant synthesis and scalability to 1000+ node systems; controversies center on cost-effectiveness of complete implementation verification versus specification-level verification for typical systems.
section_summaries.evidence-graded-findings: Strong-confidence findings include formal methods finding subtle bugs missed by testing, distributed protocol verification feasibility via EPR/decidable logic, and CRDT correctness requiring network models in formalization; moderate-confidence findings indicate verified implementations achieve within 2.4x performance of unverified equivalents and engineers learn TLA+ within 2-3 weeks without formal training background.
section_summaries.executive-summary: Formal verification research spans model checking (automation, state-space explosion), interactive theorem proving (unbounded systems, manual effort), and decidable fragments like EPR (automation without limits), with AWS production validation of TLA+ since 2011 and ongoing debate over end-to-end implementation verification cost-benefit versus specification-level verification.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Critical gaps include absence of longitudinal cost-benefit analysis comparing specification-only versus end-to-end verification in production systems, limited empirical data on invariant discovery time in interactive theorem proving, lack of guidance for choosing verification methodology by system characteristics, and sparse documentation of formal verification failure modes when assumptions about fault models or network behavior prove incorrect.
section_summaries.methodology-note: This LLM-synthesized literature review aggregates peer-reviewed sources (2002-2017) verified via WebFetch where accessible; citations require independent verification before production use; source reliability graded by venue tier (ACM/USENIX premier venues rated 5, specialized venues 4) and verification status (paywalled AWS paper
section_summaries.source-catalog: Catalog documents fifteen peer-reviewed sources ranging from 2002-2017 including foundational work (Lamport's TLA+, Raft), industrial application (AWS formal methods), and frameworks spanning Coq (Verdi), Dafny (IronFleet), Isabelle/HOL (CRDT verification), EPR (Paxos variants), and interactive tools (Ivy), with relevance ratings 4-5 and verification status documented for citation reliability.
section_summaries.thematic-synthesis: Research clusters around five themes: specification languages and tools (TLA+, Ivy, Dafny), implementation verification frameworks (Verdi, IronFleet), specific protocol verification (Paxos variants, Raft, CRDTs), fault models and refinement methodology (Byzantine, crash, network abstractions), and industrial adoption patterns showing TLA+ productivity in entry-level engineers and bug discovery rates exceeding testing/code review.

autom8y::knossos::literature-human-ai-te.domain_summary: Human-AI teaming and shared autonomy research spans four decades, establishing that trust calibration—the correspondence between operator reliance and actual system capability—is critical for preventing both automation misuse and disuse. The field centers on the "automation irony" (Bainbridge, 1983): automating easy tasks degrades human performance on residual hard tasks and monitoring work that humans do poorly. Contemporary work emphasizes dynamic, context-sensitive autonomy with transparency mechanisms mapped to operator situational awareness, challenging static regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act. METHODOLOGY-NOTE | Literature review synthesized from four decades of human factors research with emphasis on foundational aviation/nuclear studies now applied to AI oversight, noting that LLM-synthesized citations require independent verification for production use. EXECUTIVE-SUMMARY | Trust calibration prevents both over-reliance (misuse) and under-reliance (disuse) of automation through correspondence between operator trust and system capability, with the core paradox being that automated easy tasks make remaining hard tasks harder for humans while relegating them to monitoring—a task humans perform poorly. SOURCE-CATALOG | Fifteen peer-reviewed sources spanning 1978–2023 documented with verification status, relevance ratings (4–5), and key claims graded by strength, establishing consensus on four functional automation stages (information acquisition, analysis, decision selection, implementation) and mechanisms like automation complacency, bias, and skill decay. THEMATIC-SYNTHESIS | Five thematic clusters emerged: trust calibration mechanisms (display design, confidence estimation, targeted calibration cues), automation paradoxes (skill decay, complacency, monitoring failures), governance structures (three-level HCAI model: engineering reliability, managerial safety culture, independent oversight), meaningful human control conditions (tracking and tracing requirements), and human-AI teaming prerequisites (understanding, trust, accurate decision-making, proper control). EVIDENCE-GRADED-FINDINGS | Strong consensus exists on automation trust-reliance correspondence, monitoring as human weakness, decomposed automation taxonomy, and that both naive and expert users experience automation bias resistant to training; moderate evidence supports targeted trust calibration cues outperforming continuous transparency and dynamic autonomy adjustment improving performance outcomes. KNOWLEDGE-GAPS | Underexplored areas include real-time calibration validation in deployed systems, long-term skill decay trajectories in high-autonomy environments, effective regulatory operationalization of "meaningful human control" beyond liability transfer, and quantified metrics for tracing-condition verification in neural network-based systems. DOMAIN-CALIBRATION | This domain addresses the paradox that static human-in-the-loop oversight fails because monitoring is precisely what humans do poorly, requiring dynamic autonomy adjustment with transparency mapped to operator situational awareness; the field converges on trust calibration and meaningful control frameworks while diverging on regulatory implementation.

autom8y::knossos::literature-interface-a.domain_summary: Interface aesthetic perception quality examines how users rapidly form visual judgments of digital interfaces and how these judgments correlate with perceived usability. Research spanning three decades establishes that aesthetic assessments occur within 50ms, comprise multiple facets (classical/expressive or simplicity/diversity/colorfulness/craftsmanship), and strongly correlate with perceived—though not necessarily actual—usability.

section_summaries.domain-calibration: Evidence quality is strong for rapid perceptual judgment formation and attitudinal correlations, moderate for cross-cultural replication and aesthetic dimensionality, and weak for objective performance outcomes and causal mechanisms.
section_summaries.evidence-graded-findings: Strong consensus exists that aesthetic judgment occurs within 50ms, visual aesthetics is multidimensional, classical/simple aesthetics correlates more strongly with perceived usability than expressive aesthetics, and first impressions remain stable across exposure durations; moderate evidence supports cross-cultural replication and processing fluency mechanisms; weak evidence exists for objective performance improvements from aesthetics alone.
section_summaries.executive-summary: Aesthetic judgments form within 50ms, strongly correlate with perceived usability (the "what is beautiful is usable" effect), comprise distinct facets, but show mixed evidence for causally improving objective performance with unresolved questions about universality and causal directionality.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Critical unknowns include whether aesthetic improvements causally enhance objective task performance versus only perceived usability, whether aesthetic-usability relationships are universal or culturally contingent, whether beauty drives perceived usability or vice versa, and which aesthetic dimensions generalize across cultures and device types.
section_summaries.methodology-note: Studies employ varied methods including factor analysis of rating scales (Lavie/Tractinsky), controlled exposure duration experiments (Lindgaard/Tuch), behavioral econometric analysis, and large-scale crowdsourced cross-cultural preference data; verification notes indicate partial access to full texts with reliance on abstracts and secondary sources.
section_summaries.source-catalog: The domain is grounded in 15+ peer-reviewed papers including Kurosu and Kashimura's foundational 1995 ATM interface study, Lavie and Tractinsky's two-dimensional classical/expressive model, Lindgaard et al.'s 50ms judgment timing findings, and Moshagen and Thielsch's VisAWI instrument measuring simplicity/diversity/colorfulness/craftsmanship facets.
section_summaries.thematic-synthesis: Research clusters around five themes: aesthetic dimensionality (classical vs. expressive, or simplicity/diversity/colorfulness/craftsmanship), rapid judgment formation mechanics, aesthetic-usability correlations and directionality, processing fluency theory explanations, and cross-cultural variation in aesthetic preferences.

autom8y::knossos::literature-multi-agent.domain_summary: # Multi-Agent Systems for Autonomous Software Engineering This domain reviews the rapidly advancing field of LLM-based multi-agent systems applied to software engineering, synthesizing evidence from 2023-2025 peer-reviewed literature and technical reports. The core tension is that performance on SWE-bench has improved dramatically (12.5% to ~80%) through single-agent advances rather than multi-agent coordination breakthroughs, with inter-agent misalignment and coordination overhead emerging as the dominant technical barriers rather than individual agent capability limitations. DOMAIN-CALIBRATION | This is a synthetic literature review synthesizing 8 primary sources (6 peer-reviewed papers, 2 technical documents) published 2023-2025; all claims should be independently verified before production use as the field experiences rapid capability changes. METHODOLOGY-NOTE | Sources were evaluated on verification status (full-text fetch where possible), relevance ranking (1-5 scale), and claim strength grading (STRONG/MODERATE/WEAK) to enable readers to assess confidence levels in each finding. EXECUTIVE-SUMMARY | Multi-agent coordination imposes measurable performance degradation on sequential tasks despite 80% improvement in overall SWE-bench scores driven by better models and tool interfaces; inter-agent misalignment (~40% of failures) rather than individual agent capability is the dominant failure mode, and no existing system demonstrates reflexive self-improvement with cross-session institutional memory that Knossos targets. SOURCE-CATALOG | Eight sources documented: SWE-Agent (agent-computer interface design primacy), Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail (MASFT taxonomy with 1,600+ traces), Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems (17.2x error amplification in independent architectures), MetaGPT (SOP-driven document communication), ChatDev (88% executability with 39.5% quality gap), Reflexion (episodic verbal RL without cross-session persistence), OpenHands (open-source platform), and Anthropic Claude technical blog (minimal scaffolding principle). THEMATIC-SYNTHESIS | Five dominant themes emerge: (1) agent-computer interface design matters more than model capability or agent quantity, (2) multi-agent systems degrade performance on sequential reasoning 39-70% across all variants, (3) structured document-based communication outperforms unstructured dialogue, (4) inter-agent misalignment dominates over technical implementation failures, and (5) no system achieves cross-session institutional memory or reflexive self-improvement loops. EVIDENCE-GRADED-FINDINGS | Strong evidence supports that independent agent architectures amplify errors 17.2x versus centralized 4.4x, interface design is the primary performance lever (12.5% SOTA with custom ACI), SOPs reduce cascading hallucinations, and verbal reflection improves coding without weight updates; moderate evidence supports that multi-agent coordination yields diminishing returns above 45% single-agent success rate and that architecture-task alignment predicts optimal coordination strategy with R²=0.524. KNOWLEDGE-GAPS | Literature lacks empirical studies of cross-session agent learning and institutional memory persistence, comparative analysis of coordination overhead costs versus single-agent scaling, longitudinal evaluation of multi-agent systems on real-world production codebases (vs. benchmarks), and mechanistic understanding of why unstructured dialogue produces cascading hallucinations while structured SOPs do not.

autom8y::knossos::literature-open-source.domain_summary: # Literature Review: Open Source as a Development Paradigm This knowledge domain synthesizes peer-reviewed and foundational literature on open source software as a governance and incentive structure, moving beyond the licensing-centric perspective. The primary contribution is demonstrating that while open source solved code distribution, it has structurally failed to solve maintenance sustainability, with Ostrom's commons governance framework and empirical work from Eghbal, Schweik & English providing converging evidence of maintainer burnout, security vulnerabilities, and misalignment between non-rivalrous code and rivalrous maintainer attention. METHODOLOGY_NOTE | This is an LLM-synthesized literature review requiring independent verification of citations; sources are graded on verification completeness (full text vs. abstract vs. description) and relevance scores (1-5). EXECUTIVE_SUMMARY | Open source functions as a non-rivalrous commons where code availability does not deplete resources, but maintainer attention is severely rivalrous and underprovisioned, with systematic failures in solving the maintenance problem despite solving distribution. SOURCE_CATALOG | Seven primary sources catalogued spanning 1990-2021 include Ostrom's foundational commons governance theory, Eghbal's reframing of open source as creator economy, Schweik & English's empirical IAD framework application, Lerner & Tirole's incentive economics, contemporary motivation surveys, and Raymond's influential but critiqued bazaar model. THEMATIC_SYNTHESIS | Open source governance models (BDFL, foundation-backed, corporate-sponsored) each exhibit documented failure modes; the fundamental structural tension is that code is non-rivalrous but maintainer labor is rivalrous and systematically underfunded relative to extracted economic value. EVIDENCE_GRADED_FINDINGS | Strong consensus evidence supports asymmetric maintainer burden claims and the disconnect between economic value extraction and maintainer compensation; moderate evidence supports career signaling and reputation as primary incentives, with empirical variation across project lifecycle stages. KNOWLEDGE_GAPS | The literature lacks systematic analysis of how open source maintenance could be provisioned at sustainable scale; cross-cultural and geographic variation in contributor motivations remains understudied; longitudinal data on how governance structures adapt post-failure is sparse. DOMAIN_CALIBRATION | This domain represents converging empirical and theoretical consensus (commons governance framework, maintainer burnout, distribution-vs.-maintenance problem split) with remaining disagreement on scalable solutions and whether current paradigm is fundamentally repairable versus requiring institutional restructuring.

autom8y::knossos::literature-organizatio.domain_summary: Organizational learning in software engineering synthesizes management theory (SECI model, psychological safety, double-loop learning) with empirical software practice research (DORA, resilience engineering), establishing that learning culture drives delivery performance and requires psychological safety as a prerequisite. The domain addresses how tacit knowledge (incident insights, operational patterns) converts to explicit artifacts (runbooks, postmortems) and why blameless cultures enable this conversion while blame-based cultures suppress reporting. EXECUTIVE-SUMMARY | Strong consensus that high-performing software organizations treat learning as strategic investment; psychological safety is prerequisite; SECI model maps incident-to-artifact knowledge conversion; controversies center on knowledge repository utilization and postmortem sufficiency; strongest evidence for DORA/Accelerate delivery-culture linkage and Edmondson's psychological safety construct. SOURCE-CATALOG | Catalogs eight primary sources: Nonaka/Takeuchi's SECI model (1995), Edmondson's psychological safety empirical foundation (1999), DORA's Accelerate research linking learning culture to delivery performance (2018), Cook's complex systems failure framework (1998), Argyris/Schön's single/double-loop learning theory (1996), Dekker's just culture distinction between retributive and restorative justice (2007), Senge's five disciplines learning organization model (1990), and Wenger's communities-of-practice social learning theory (1998). THEMATIC-SYNTHESIS | Integrates tacit-to-explicit knowledge conversion, psychological safety as learning enabler, single-loop versus double-loop learning depth, blameless postmortem design for incident analysis, defensive routines as learning barriers, community-of-practice knowledge dissemination, and resilience-engineering rejection of root-cause mythology. EVIDENCE-GRADED-FINDINGS | Strongest evidence supports psychological safety predicting learning behavior, DORA's statistical linkage of learning climate to deployment frequency/MTTR/change failure rate, and complex systems failing through multiple insufficient causes; moderate evidence supports SECI model applicability and Westrum culture typology; weakest evidence supports middle-up-down management optimality and team learning as fundamental unit. KNOWLEDGE-GAPS | Limited empirical research on knowledge repository effectiveness without cultural reinforcement, sparse direct application of SECI model to software-specific contexts, insufficient data on blameless postmortem ROI versus cost, under-researched mechanisms of defensive routine dissolution, and minimal longitudinal studies tracking learning culture interventions in software organizations. DOMAIN-CALIBRATION | Domain synthesizes 1990s-2000s organizational theory with 2010s-2020s empirical software engineering research; primarily English-language sources; stronger evidence from manufacturing/healthcare domains than pure software engineering; applicable to organizations >50 people; assumes distributed knowledge work rather than individual-contributor contexts. METHODOLOGY-NOTE | LLM-synthesized review with independently-verified citations; full-text verification achieved for Cook (2000) and Edmondson (1999); publisher catalog verification for Nonaka, Argyris/Schön, Senge, Wenger, Dekker; content known from secondary citations and DORA research program; recommendations require independent verification before production decisions.

autom8y::knossos::literature-production-.domain_summary: Google's cluster management evolution (Borg, Omega, Kubernetes, Autopilot) demonstrates production self-tuning through layered architectural separation: static heuristic schedulers tuned by engineers handle task placement, while ML-based feedback loops in Autopilot continuously optimize per-job resource allocations using ensemble models. Self-tuning capability progressed from Borg's simple exponential decay resource estimators to Autopilot's multi-model approach selecting optimal parameters, achieving 23% resource slack versus 46% for manual allocation while reducing OOM incidents by 10x. EXECUTIVE-SUMMARY | Self-tuning in Google's systems is a layered architecture separating policy (human-controlled priority bands, quotas, preemption rules) from optimization (automated resource reservations, vertical CPU/memory scaling, horizontal job scaling via ML ensembles). SOURCE-CATALOG | The corpus includes five peer-reviewed papers spanning Borg (monolithic scheduler with exponential-decay resource reclamation), Omega (shared-state architecture with optimistic concurrency control), Autopilot (triple closed-loop ML-based autoscaling achieving 99.5% zero-OOM rate), and longitudinal workload analysis confirming effectiveness of automatic vertical scaling across eight years of production data. THEMATIC-ANALYSIS | Borg measures fine-grained per-task CPU/memory usage every few seconds with 300-second startup transient buffering, while Autopilot enriches signals with OOM cumulative distributions, resource slack percentages, and exponentially-smoothed overrun/underrun costs feeding ensemble recommenders that select optimal decay rates and safety margins; humans retain control of priority bands, preemption invariants, quota allocation, and scoring function design while the system automatically tunes resource reservations, vertical limits, and horizontal scaling. autom8y::knossos::literature-recursive-s.domain_summary: Recursive self-improvement (RSI) in AI systems has evolved from theoretical frameworks (Godel Machines, AIXI) to practical implementations (Darwin Godel Machine, STaR) that demonstrate bounded capability gains while encountering natural limitations including model collapse and diminishing returns. The literature bifurcates into theoretical optimality studies proving RSI is possible but computationally intractable, practical engineering work showing 20-30% improvements on benchmarks, and safety research developing formal drift detection and alignment monitoring mechanisms. Current consensus treats RSI as a concrete systems problem requiring evaluation tooling rather than existential risk speculation.

section_summaries.executive-summary: Unbounded optimal RSI is theoretically possible but computationally intractable; practical bounded systems achieve 20-50% improvements on code benchmarks while facing model collapse, diminishing returns, and sublinear scaling; consensus emerging at ICLR 2026 treats RSI as requiring evaluation tooling and alignment monitoring rather than speculative futurism.

section_summaries.source-catalog: Eight foundational and contemporary sources spanning theoretical frameworks (Godel Machines, AIXI, intelligence explosion), practical implementations (Darwin Godel Machine, STaR, Godel Agent, Absolute Zero), and safety mechanisms (SAHOO drift detection) establish the evolving landscape of recursive self-improvement research.

autom8y::knossos::literature-self-adapti.domain_summary: Self-adaptive and self-modifying software systems research spans from Dijkstra's 1974 self-stabilization theory to modern autonomic computing frameworks, converging on the MAPE-K (Monitor-Analyze-Plan-Execute-Knowledge) feedback loop as the canonical architecture for systems that manage their own adaptation. The field addresses the challenge of managing escalating software complexity through systems with explicit separation between adaptation logic and managed components, requiring formal assurance mechanisms to guarantee stability properties like convergence and closure. EXECUTIVE-SUMMARY | Self-adaptive systems have a 20+ year lineage from IBM's 2003 autonomic vision and Dijkstra's 1974 self-stabilization theory, with MAPE-K feedback loops and explicit architectural separation as proven prerequisites for stability, though centralized vs. decentralized control, runtime verification scalability, and formal assurance tractability remain open controversies. SOURCE-CATALOG | The domain draws authoritative sources from five foundational works: Kephart & Chess (2003) on autonomic computing properties, Dijkstra (1974) on self-stabilization convergence/closure guarantees, Cheng et al. (2009) establishing SEAMS research directions, Garlan et al. (2004) on Rainbow's architecture-based adaptation framework, and Weyns et al. (2013) defining five decentralization control patterns. autom8y::knossos::literature-ui-design-e.domain_summary: UI design engineering represents a unified craft practice that collapses traditional design-to-implementation handoffs by treating code as the primary design medium, as exemplified by practitioners at Linear, Vercel, and Notion. This discipline emphasizes obsessive attention to interaction details (physics-based motion, invisible micro-interactions, performance thresholds), rejection of static mockup deliverables in favor of live-application iteration, and development of intuition through direct implementation and reflection-through-making rather than theoretical study or data-driven processes.

section_summaries.domain-calibration: This domain represents early-stage practitioner-led knowledge with strong qualitative richness but minimal scientific rigor; findings should inform exploration rather than prescriptive practice adoption without organizational validation and team capability assessment.
section_summaries.evidence-graded-findings: Most claims carry WEAK or UNVERIFIED confidence tiers due to reliance on self-reported practitioner essays and interviews lacking peer-reviewed empirical validation; foundational HCI principles (Fitts's Law, accessibility standards) are MODERATE-to-STRONG, while workflow and team-scale claims remain largely unvalidated.
section_summaries.executive-summary: UI design engineering unifies design sensibility and engineering implementation into a single code-mediated workflow, with key controversies around whether hybrid expertise produces superior outcomes versus diluting specialization, and whether practices generalizing beyond small high-trust teams.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Research lacks longitudinal studies comparing design-engineering outcomes across team sizes, empirical validation of 200ms animation thresholds, controlled comparisons of code-first versus mockup-first workflows, and evidence distinguishing whether superior Linear/Vercel outcomes stem from craft practices or organizational factors like team selection and scope discipline.
section_summaries.methodology-note: This review synthesizes practitioner essays, interviews, and reference materials (2020-2024) from design-forward technology companies, employing confidence grading based on citation independence and empirical support rather than peer review.
section_summaries.source-catalog: Primary sources include practitioner essays and interviews from Rauno Freiberg (Vercel), Karri Saarinen (Linear), Paco Coursey (Linear), and Brian Lovin (Notion) documenting implementation-first workflows, interaction design principles grounded in real-world physics metaphors, and minimal design systems where the running application serves as canonical design source.
section_summaries.thematic-synthesis: Core themes encompass interaction design principles (interruptibility, momentum, Fitts's Law application), implementation-level polish (200ms animation ceilings, optimistic updates, native HTML forms), rejection of traditional design-engineering handoffs in favor of code-first prototyping, and craft development through direct prototype iteration rather than passive study or A/B testing.

autom8y::knossos::radar.domain_summary: This knowledge domain tracks technical debt, architecture violations, and systemic failure patterns in the autom8y::knossos codebase through convergent radar signals. The 18 identified opportunities span CLI output bypass, error handling drift, layer architecture violations, and recurring silent failure patterns primarily clustered in the materialize pipeline and CC integration boundaries. Four design constraints have been silently resolved but remain marked as active, creating stale knowledge artifacts. SUMMARY: Convergent signal detection across 7 radar types identifying 18 opportunities, with strongest signal in materialize pipeline and untested CLI entry points flagged by three independent decay signals. OPPORTUNITIES: 14 documented opportunities ranging from HIGH (os.Stdout bypass in agent commands, fmt.Errorf at CLI boundaries, Layer 3→2 import violations, silent failure/observability patterns, integration failure patterns, untested CLI entry points, non-atomic writes) to MEDIUM (RISK-004 os.Remove without error propagation, stale knowledge, KnossosHome cache poisoning in tests, schema evolution patterns, data corruption patterns, historical debt patterns) severity. SIGNALS-WITH-NO-FINDINGS: Radar signal coverage confirmed complete with no gaps between signal definitions and opportunity mappings. SUPPRESSED-FINDINGS: Opportunity density thresholded at 0.65 minimum confidence; lower-confidence findings withheld to maintain signal-to-noise ratio above 1.2. ADVISORY: Focus area prioritization recommends inscription rollback smoke tests and manifest validate tests (highest consequence), followed by agent validate command refactoring (high user-facing payoff), then org/knows package fmt.Errorf cleanup via CI gate to prevent future drift. METHODOLOGY: Signals computed via cross-frequency analysis of unguarded scars, convention drift, architecture decay, recurring scars, and constraint violations; confidence intervals calculated as product of signal strength (0.78–0.88) and finding specificity (0.80–0.98); SCARs classified into 6 systemic patterns (silent failure, integration failure, schema evolution, data corruption, historical debt, test isolation).

autom8y::knossos::release/dependency-top.domain_summary: Knossos is a standalone Go module with no downstream dependents, using git tags as its version source and triggered GoReleaser binary distribution on tag pushes. The single-repo release topology requires minimal coordination with no cross-repo DAG or auto-escalation triggers, making PATCH-level complexity sufficient for releases. DEPENDENCY-TOPOLOGY | Knossos is a single-repo Go module (github.com/autom8y/knossos) with no downstream dependents, using git tags for versioning and GoReleaser for binary distribution on tag pushes, requiring no cascading version bumps or cross-repo coordination.

autom8y::knossos::release/history.domain_summary: Knossos release history tracks the evolution of autom8y's Ari CLI tool from v0.3.0 through v0.12.0+, documenting release verdicts, execution timings, and feature milestones across GoReleaser-based CI/CD pipelines. The knowledge domain demonstrates a mature release process with 24+ consecutive PASS verdicts, consistent ~150-190s chain times, and multi-platform binary distribution via Homebrew. release-log | Detailed release records from v0.7.0 onwards including commit counts, workflow execution times (release.yml ~100-130s, e2e-distribution.yml ~48-61s), asset verification (5/5 binary + checksums), and Homebrew tap update dispatch latency (1-4s). historical-summary-v030--v0312 | Early stabilization period (2026-03-03 to 2026-03-05) progressing from PARTIAL verdicts with PAT/e2e dispatch fixes to first PASS at v0.3.2, then establishing consistent PASS pattern through cache-only patches and boundary migrations (.claude/ → .knossos/). historical-summary-v040--v061 | Foundation-building period (2026-03-05 to 2026-03-07) introducing ari agent embody, perspective layers with simulate mode, session schema v2.3, and CLI UX remediation while maintaining ~150-166s chain times and 100% PASS verdict rate.

autom8y::knossos::release/platform-profi.domain_summary: The autom8y/knossos project is a Go module distributed as a cross-platform binary (ari) via GoReleaser v2, with builds targeting darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, and linux/arm64. The repository uses a two-stage release pipeline triggered by version tags (v*) that publishes releases and triggers end-to-end distribution testing via GitHub Actions. REPOSITORY-ECOSYSTEM-MAP | The knossos repository is a Go module (github.com/autom8y/knossos) distributed as GoReleaser v2 binaries across four platforms, published to autom8y/homebrew-tap via Homebrew, with asset naming pattern ari_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz and build orchestration via Justfile and Makefile targets. PIPELINE-CHAIN-DISCOVERY | The release pipeline is a single-repo trigger chain with two stages: release.yml (triggered by v* tags, classification: build) flows into e2e-distribution.yml (triggered by release.published, classification: deploy), with the distribution workflow serving as the terminal health-check stage. CI-WORKFLOWS | Five CI workflows automate testing and deployment: ariadne-tests.yml, validate-orchestrators.yml, and verify-doctrine.yml run on pull requests/pushes; release.yml triggers on version tags; e2e-distribution.yml validates distribution on release publish; and deploy-clew.yml deploys to main branch via push. KNOWN-ISSUES | The deploy-clew.yml workflow has a critical AWS OIDC configuration bug (empty account ID in role ARN); four Dependabot vulnerability alerts exist on main (informational); and Go minimum version was bumped to 1.25 in v0.16.0 due to slack-go dependency requirements.

autom8y::knossos::scar-tissue.domain_summary: # Autom8y Knossos Scar Tissue This knowledge domain documents 38 failure modes discovered in the Knossos codebase, ranging from race conditions and data corruption to integration failures and schema evolution issues. Each failure includes root cause analysis, specific fix locations in the codebase, regression tests, and defensive patterns to prevent recurrence. The catalog serves as a historical record of production bugs and their mitigations across file I/O, session management, materialization, linting, and hook execution. ---

section_summaries.agent-relevance-tagging: Agents-specific failures tracked across SCAR-005 (destructive RemoveAll), SCAR-017 (skill-name anti-pattern in prompts), SCAR-018 (context fork blocking task access), SCAR-021 (cross-rite scope materialization), SCAR-027 (ephemeral skills in shared Mena), and SCAR-034 (ghost agent propagation via standing registry).
section_summaries.category-coverage: Integration failures dominate with 14 entries, followed by data corruption (7), configuration drift (6), schema evolution (3), performance issues (2), historical boundaries (2), and testing constraints (2), with no discovered memory leaks, SQL errors, authentication failures, network timeouts, or infinite loops.
section_summaries.defensive-pattern-documentation: Defensive patterns include atomic file locking (SCAR-001), selective writes from managed sets (SCAR-005), lint enforcement for schema separation (SCAR-007), timeout guards on subprocesses (SCAR-010), `NormalizeStatus()` at read sites (SCAR-014), and naming contract validation (SCAR-034) to prevent ghost entities.
section_summaries.failure-catalog-completeness: The catalog contains 34 numbered SCAR entries (SCAR-001 through SCAR-034), 3 unnumbered GO entries for content rewriting bypasses, and 1 writeguard path traversal entry, with fixes verified against codebase commit a73d68a6 and regression tests in `scar_regression_test.go`, `hooks_test.go`, and related test files.
section_summaries.fix-location-mapping: All primary fixes mapped to specific source files including `internal/lock/lock.go`, `internal/materialize/materialize.go`, `internal/cmd/hook/writeguard.go`, `internal/cmd/lint/lint.go`, and `internal/session/status.go`, with compound fixes spanning 4+ files for SCAR-009, SCAR-010, SCAR-012, and GO-001/002/003.
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: SCAR-029 (Moirai template `${SESSION_ID}`) lacks formal number and regression test; Go 1.23 workaround (SCAR-030) uses dependency injection instead of `t.Chdir`; materialize test I/O bottleneck (SCAR-031) identified but not a bug; config canonicalization (SCAR-033) requires operational discipline rather than code fix.

autom8y::knossos::test-coverage.domain_summary: autom8y::knossos::test-coverage provides a comprehensive analysis of a Go codebase's test coverage, identifying 84.3% package-level coverage (86 of 102 packages tested) with 3,860 test functions across 280 test files. The document maps critical testing gaps, establishes testing conventions, and prioritizes coverage improvements for security-critical paths like HMAC signing and session lifecycle operations.

section_summaries.coverage-gaps: The codebase has 16 untested packages primarily in CLI wrappers and Slack/Clew development areas, with high-risk blind spots including `internal/cmd/hook/call.go` (HMAC signing), `internal/concept` (fuzzy matching), and `internal/cmd/session/park.go` (session lifecycle).
section_summaries.knowledge-gaps: Documentation lacks actual line-level coverage percentages, criticality assessment of certain untested files like `cheapo_revert.go`, design rationale for omitting `t.Parallel()` in session tests, and granular error-path coverage metrics beyond the `wantErr` pattern signal.
section_summaries.test-structure-summary: The 86 tested packages concentrate heavily in materialization pipeline (31 test files), session management (15-27 test files), search/Clew (full coverage), and Sails quality gates (full coverage), while session lifecycle operations (`park.go`, `resume.go`), harness adapters, and Slack components remain untested.
section_summaries.testing-conventions: Tests use dominant patterns of `TestFunctionName_Scenario` naming, table-driven subtests via `t.Run` (128 files), stdlib assertions (`t.Errorf`), `t.TempDir()` for filesystem isolation (153 files), and `t.Parallel()` for concurrency (83 files), with integration tests distinguished by `_integration_test.go` suffix rather than build tags.
